sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=30, col=10).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 45, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves green pixel right 2px within container (VERIFIED step 3)', 'LEFT': 'Moves green pixel left 2px within container (VERIFIED step 4)', 'UP': 'Moves green up within container if space exists (VERIFIED). No portal swap ever occurred - previous claim was wrong.', 'DOWN': 'Moves green down within container if space exists (VERIFIED)', 'MOUSE': 'Clicking on green pixel causes HUD timer bar to shrink by 1 pixel (VERIFIED step 42). Second click has no additional effect.'}
- Object taxonomy: {'green_pixel': 'Active 2x2 green (N) object, controlled by arrow keys', 'divider_blocks': 'White 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, vertical line', 'colored_pixels': '2x2 objects in left containers: yellow, blue, red, dark red, green', 'hud_timer': 'White bar at row 63, shrinks when MOUSE clicks green pixel'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'White horizontal bar at row 63, shrinks as timer/progress indicator. Decreases by 1 pixel per MOUSE click on green.', 'divider': 'Vertical line of white 2x2 blocks at col 32-33'}
Current level:
- World model: Two-panel layout. Left (charcoal bg) has gray containers with colored 2x2 pixels. Right (black bg) has white containers with red/blue shapes. Green pixel is the active object, moves within bottom container (rows 38-43, cols 8-13). MOUSE click on green shrinks the timer bar by 1. The timer bar started at ~44 pixels and is now ~43. This suggests the game is about clicking green a certain number of times, or clicking green after positioning it in specific locations.
- Goal model: The timer bar shrinks when clicking green. Goal may be to shrink it to zero, or to click green at specific positions to trigger color transfers to the right side. Need to explore whether clicking green at different positions or clicking other colored pixels has different effects.
- Recent findings: ['MOUSE on green at (42,12) caused board_changed=True (step 42)', 'Change was only in HUD timer bar: white bar at row 63 shrunk by 1 pixel', 'Second MOUSE click on green had no effect (step 43)', 'No arrow key movement has ever caused board_changed=True']
- Open questions: ['Does clicking green at different positions have different effects?', 'Does clicking other colored pixels (yellow, blue, red) do anything?', 'Is the goal to shrink the timer to zero, or to trigger color transfers?', 'Do I need to move green to different containers before clicking?']
- Plan: Try MOUSE on other colored pixels (yellow, blue, red) to see if they have different effects. Also try moving green to different positions and clicking there.
- Strategy log: ['Abandoned portal swap theory: no action ever caused board_changed until MOUSE on green (step 42)', 'Movement actions (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT) never cause board changes - only MOUSE does']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(20,24) on yellow pixel: no selection change (step 9)', 'UP at cols 8-9 rows 38-39: no change (step 16)', 'UP at cols 10-11 rows 38-39: no change (step 17)', 'RIGHT at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no movement (step 13)', 'UP at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no change (step 12)', 'UP at cols 12-13 rows 38-39: no move (step 37)', 'RIGHT from cols 12-13 rows 42-43: no move', 'MOUSE on green second time: no additional effect (step 43)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 44 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on red at (30, 10) did NOT cause board_changed. So clicking on red doesn't work. But clicking on green and yellow did work. What's the difference?
Looking at the colored pixels:
- Yellow: at [[20, 24], [20, 25], [21, 25], [21, 24]] - this is a 2x2 yellow pixel
- Green: at [[42, 12], [42, 13], [43, 13], [43, 12]] - this is a 2x2 green pixel
- Red: I clicked at (30, 10) but it didn't work
Wait, let me look at the segmentation output more carefully. The only 2x2 colored pixel (excluding green N) was yellow. That means the red, blue, and dark red objects are NOT 2x2 pixels - they might be different sizes or shapes.
Let me look at the middle container more carefully. From the image, I can see:
- Top container: blue 2x2 + yellow 2x2
- Middle container: gray + red + dark red (these might be larger or different shapes)
- Bottom container: green 2x2
So maybe only the 2x2 pixels (yellow and green) can be clicked to shrink the timer. The red, blue, and dark red objects might be different sizes.
Let me check the segmentation to find all colored objects and their sizes. I need to find what the red object actually is.
Actually, wait. Looking at the output from earlier, the red pixel was listed as a 2x2 object. Let me re-examine. In the first segmentation output, I saw:
- Y: [[20, 24], [20, 25], [21, 25], [21, 24]], pixels=4
But I didn't see a red 2x2 object. Let me look at the middle container more carefully. From the ascii view, the middle container has:
- Gray area
- Red area
- Dark red area
These might be larger than 2x2. Let me check the segmentation to find all non-background, non-divider objects.
Actually, I think the key insight is that only certain colored pixels can be clicked. Green and yellow worked, but red didn't. Maybe the game is about clicking specific pixels in a specific order.
Let me try clicking on the blue pixel (the 2x2 one in the top container) to see if it works.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Red click didn't work. Let me find ALL colored objects and their sizes to understand what's clickable.

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All non-background, non-divider objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['W', 'G', 'c', 'B']:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# The blue 2x2 is at [[20, 18], [20, 21], [23, 21], [23, 18]] with 16 pixels - that's a 4x4 blue object!
# Let me try clicking on the blue 4x4 object
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 21, 'col': 19}])
print(f"\nAfter blue click: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}")

# Check timer
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and 30 < n['pixels'] < 100:
        print(f"Timer: {n['pixels']}")
        break
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves green right 2px", "LEFT": "Moves green left 2px", "UP": "Moves green up if space", "DOWN": "Moves green down if space", "MOUSE": "Clicking on some colored pixels shrinks timer by 1 (green, yellow work; red didn't)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"green_pixel": "Active 2x2 green object", "divider_blocks": "White 2x2 at col 32-33", "colored_pixels": "Various colored objects in left containers"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "White bar row 63, timer that shrinks by 1 per click on certain pixels"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Clicking green and yellow shrinks timer. Clicking red did NOT work. Need to understand which pixels are clickable.", "goal_model": "Figure out which colored pixels can be clicked and in what order", "recent_findings": ["Green click: timer 44->43", "Yellow click: timer 43->42", "Red click: no effect"], "open_questions": ["What makes green and yellow clickable but not red?", "Is it about pixel size (2x2 only)?", "Does clicking blue work?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking blue object, then examine all colored objects to find pattern", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["Second click on green: no effect", "Click on red: no effect"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
